Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tax Costs As To Defendant Cyprus Amax Minerals Company
Off Calendar Jun-21-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance and tentative ruling adopted: Plaintiff's motion to tax costs as to defendant Cyprus Amax Minerals Company is granted in part and denied in part. Of the $12,494.75 claimed for expert witness costs, $11,289.75 is taxed and defendant may recover $1,205. Plaintiff did not accept defendant's November 9, 2016 section 998 offer to compromise and failed to obtain a more favorable judgment or award. This constitutes prima facie evidence showing defendant's offer was reasonable and that defendant is eligible for costs. (Elrod v. Oregon Cummins Diesel, Inc. (1987) 195 Cal.App.3d 692, 700.) Plaintiff failed to meet her burden of demonstrating that defendant's offer was a "token" or bad faith offer. $11,289.75 is taxed because there is no showing that the $1,843.75 in costs for RJ Lee Group and the $9,446 in costs for Exponent, Inc. are postoffer costs. (C.C.P. 998(c)(1).) Defendant's documents demonstrate that the $1,205 in costs for expert Laura Dolan was incurred post-offer. The motion is granted as to Item 13 - "Other" costs of $789.68, which are taxed in their entirety. These costs are either not allowed pursuant to C.C.P. 1033.5(b) or do not meet the requirements of C.C.P. 1033.5(a)(13), or, even if they are allowed, there has been no showing they are reasonably necessary to the conduct of the litigation rather than merely convenient or beneficial to its preparation pursuant to C.C.P. 1033.5(c). Plaintiff has withdrawn her motion as to Item 1 (filing and motion fees). Prevailing party to prepare a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, matter not reported (503/CML)

RULING: PLAINTIFF MARY LYONS Notice Of Motion And Motion Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure Section 1008(A) For An Order Granting Reconsideration Of The November 30, 2016 Order On Defendant Colgate-Palmolive Companys MSJ MSA, And For A New Order Denying That Motion.

Matter called, argument held. The Court, having considered the arguments of counsel, adopted the tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff's motion pursuant to C.C.P. 1008(a) for an order granting reconsideration of the November 30, 2016 order on defendant Colgate-Palmolive Company's MSJ/MSA, and for a new order denying that motion is denied. Plaintiff did not demonstrate that the deposition testimony of Dr. Ronald Gordon on which she relies constitutes new or different facts within the meaning of section 1008(a) nor did plaintiff provide a satisfactory explanation for the failure to produce the evidence at an earlier time. (C.C.P. 1008(a), New York Times Co. v. Superior Court (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 206, Shiffer v. CBS Corp. (2015) 240 Cal.App.4th 246.) Plaintiff's request that the Court enter an order sua sponte without relying on section 1008 is denied. Off the record, the Court reviewed the proposed order and proposed judgment in chambers. Back on the record, Ms. Bartlett's objections are stated for the record. Argument/discussion held. At the conclusion of argument, the Court signed the order and the judgment. Judge: Garrett L. Wong, Court Reporter: Kimberly L. Avery, CSR # 5074 of Aiken Welch (877.451.1580). (503/GLW)

Off Calendar Nov-22-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT CYPRUS AMAX MINERALS COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT

Appearances: Nectaria Belantis and Lisa M. Barley of Simon Greenstone Panatier Bartlett (562.590.3400) for plaintiff and responding party; Jennifer Lee of Dentons US LLP (415.267.4126) for defendant and moving party

Argued and the Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: denied. Defendant failed to sustain its initial burden under either prong of Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826. Defendant failed to demonstrate that plaintiff does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence that she was exposed to asbestos-containing products or materials attributable to defendant. Defendant did not support its motion with evidence that plaintiff failed to provide meaningful responses to comprehensive interrogatories designed to elicit all the evidence plaintiff has to support her contention of liability, precluding defendant from sustaining its burden under the factually devoid prong of Aguilar. Defendant also failed to sustain its burden of presenting evidence that conclusively negates an element of plaintiff's causes of action. The evidence submitted by defendant, namely the declaration of Patrick Downey and attached Standard Test Method document, does not negate an element of plaintiff's claims. Specifically, it does not demonstrate that talc supplied by Cyprus (or an alternate entity) during the relevant timeframe did not contain asbestos. Plaintiff's objection no. 1 is overruled. While defendant's briefs make arguments related to successor liability and substantial factor causation, those issues were not properly raised by defendant's moving papers. Order signed. Judge: Garrett L. Wong, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Carol Haraburda, CSR 8052 (Tel: 925.360.6065)

RULING:
SPECIALLY APPEARING DEFENDANT WHITTAKER, CLARK & DANIELS, INC.'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS FOR LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION

Hearing held and tentative ruling adopted: Specially appearing defendant Whittaker, Clark & Daniels, Inc.'s motion to quash service of summons for lack of personal jurisdiction is granted. Plaintiff did not claim general jurisdiction and plaintiff failed to meet her burden of demonstrating that this Court may exercise specific jurisdiction over defendant. Even assuming plaintiff demonstrated that defendant has purposefully directed its activities at California, plaintiff failed to demonstrate that her claims arise out of or are related to these forum-directed activities. Because plaintiff failed to meet her initial burden, defendant had no burden to show that the exercise of jurisdiction would be unreasonable. Defendant's objections to the evidence submitted with plaintiff's initial opposition are overruled as to Exhibits B, D and E and sustained as to Exhibits C and F. Defendant's objections to the evidence submitted with plaintiff's supplemental opposition are overruled as to Exhibit C and sustained as to Exhibits D and E. Order signed in open court. Nectaria Belantis of SIMON GREENSTONE PANATIER BARTLETT, P.C. (562-590-3400), appearing via CourtCall for plaintiff / opposing party Mary Lyons

RULING:
DEFENDANT BRENNTAG SPECIALTIES, INC.'S MOTION TO QUASH FOR LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION

Hearing held and tentative ruling adopted: Specially appearing defendant Brenntag Specialties, Inc.'s ("BSI") motion to quash for lack of personal jurisdiction is granted. Plaintiff did not claim general jurisdiction and plaintiff failed to meet her burden of demonstrating that this Court may exercise specific jurisdiction over defendant. Plaintiff asserts that defendant is successor-in-interest to Whittaker, Clark & Daniels, Inc. ("WCD") but plaintiff failed to demonstrate specific jurisdiction as to WCD for the reasons stated in the order granting WCD's motion to quash. However, even if plaintiff had demonstrated that this Court may exercise specific jurisdiction over WCD, plaintiff still did not demonstrate that this Court may exercise specific jurisdiction over BSI because plaintiff did not demonstrate that BSI is successor-in-interest to WCD or that BSI assumed WCD's liabilities. Defendant's objections to the evidence submitted with plaintiff's initial opposition are overruled as to Exhibits B, D and E and sustained as to Exhibits C and F. Defendant's objections to the evidence submitted with plaintiff's supplemental opposition are overruled as to Exhibit C and sustained as to Exhibits D and E. Order signed in open court. Hearing held and tentative ruling adopted as amended: Plaintiff's motion for preference is granted pursuant to C.C.P. 36(d), (e). Trial date is December 5, 2016 at 11:15am in Department 503. Last day pursuant to C.C.P. 36(f) is December 21, 2016. Local Rule 20 shall be followed by the parties as well as all other San Francisco Local Rules and California Rules of Court. The last day for hearing summary judgment/adjudication motions is November 22, 2016. Summary judgment/adjudication motions shall be brought on 40 days notice, with oppositions due 10 days before the hearing and replies due 5 days before the hearing. The Court will allow a maximum of four summary judgment/adjudication motions per day to be calendared in this action. Before a party files and serves such motion, it must contact the Court's Calendar Clerk to make a reservation. Time to respond to written discovery that has not yet been served is shortened to 20 days. For written discovery that has already been served, responses are due within 20 days of this hearing or by the date determined by the C.C.P., whichever is earlier. If there is any kind of issue/dispute that requires meet and confer, such meet and confer shall not be made via email or letter. It shall occur in person or via telephone. Plaintiff to make a settlement demand on defendants no later than October 14, 2016. Responses to the demand are due no later than October 28, 2016. The parties are ordered to provide confidential settlement demands stating their best and final offers. These confidential demands must be submitted to Pang Ly by November 28, 2016 at 2:00p.m. Settlement conference in room 603 at 9:30a.m. on November 30, 2016. Fact discovery cut-off date is November 23, 2016. Expert discovery cut-off date is December 2, 2016. Electronic service is considered the equivalent of personal service. All bankruptcy documents shall be turned over to defendants no later than September 23, 2016.
